caption: Fig. 1 e Initial microstructure of FeeMneAleC steel.
citation_sentences: The material used in the experiment is FeeMneAleC steel and its main chemical composition (mass fraction, %) is: 1.46 C, 25.14 Mn, 10 Al, 0.053 Nb and the rest is Fe. The corresponding initial microstructure is shown in Fig. 1. The forging billet was processed into a cylindrical sample of F 8 mm 12 mm, and the perpendicularity of both ends of the samples was ensured. The isothermal constant strain rate compression experiments were carried out with the Gleeble-3500 thermo-mechanical simulator. The experimental temperatures were 900, 950, 1000, 1050, 1100 and $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , and the strain rates were 0.01, 0.1, 1 and $1 0 \ { \mathsf { s } } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively. The maximum compression was 60% (corresponding to the maximum true strain 0.92). The mica foils with high-temperature lubricants were affixed to the two end faces of the sample to reduce the friction and the non-uniformity of deformation. The sample was heated to 1200 -C at a rate of ${ 1 0 ~ ^ { \circ } } \mathrm { C } / { \mathsf { s } }$ , then homogenized by holding for 120 s, and finally cooled to the set T at a rate of 5 -C/s. The sample was for 15 s at the set T before compression, so the temperature difference between the inside and outside of the sample was the same. After compression, the sample was sprayed with water to cool the steel down. Figure 2 shows the experimental process. The compressed sample was split in half along the axial axis, and after sample preparation, grinding, polishing and etching, the microstructure images were collected using a LEXT OLS4000 laser confocal microscope. The etching solution was: 30% nitric acid alcohol solution (volume fraction). Through the Gleeble-3500 thermomechanical simulator, the isothermal constant strain rate thermal compression experiments of the same FeeMneAleC steel batch with the same composition can be confirmed under the same experimental conditions in order to check the reproducibility of the proposed system.

caption: Fig. 2 e Compression experimental process.
citation_sentences: The material used in the experiment is FeeMneAleC steel and its main chemical composition (mass fraction, %) is: 1.46 C, 25.14 Mn, 10 Al, 0.053 Nb and the rest is Fe. The corresponding initial microstructure is shown in Fig. 1. The experimental temperatures were 900, 950, 1000, 1050, 1100 and $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , and the strain rates were 0.01, 0.1, 1 and $1 0 \ { \mathsf { s } } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively. The maximum compression was 60% (corresponding to the maximum true strain 0.92). The mica foils with high-temperature lubricants were affixed to the two end faces of the sample to reduce the friction and the non-uniformity of deformation. The sample was heated to 1200 -C at a rate of ${ 1 0 ~ ^ { \circ } } \mathrm { C } / { \mathsf { s } }$ , then homogenized by holding for 120 s, and finally cooled to the set T at a rate of 5 -C/s. The sample was for 15 s at the set T before compression, so the temperature difference between the inside and outside of the sample was the same. Fig. 4 e Relationship maps of lnε\_  ln½sinhðaspÞ (a) and ln½sinhðaspÞ  1000=T (b).
citation_sentences: Take the natural logarithm on both sides of formula (1), and the relationship between $\ln [ \sinh ( \alpha \sigma _ { \mathrm { p } } ) ] - 1 0 0 0 / T$ and lnε\_ $\ln [ \sinh ( \alpha \sigma _ { \mathfrak { p } } ) ]$ can be established, as shown in Fig. 4. According to the linear fitting, the average slopes of the curves in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) are $k _ { 1 } = 5 . 1 3 5$ and $k _ { 2 } = 1 5 . 6 9 0$ , respectively. Furthermore, the dynamic recrystallization activation energy of $\mathrm { F e { \mathrm { - } } M n { \mathrm { - } } A l { \mathrm { - } } C }$ steel is 669.844 kJ $\mathrm { m o l } ^ { - 1 }$ according to formula (2):

The parallelism of the fitted straight line in Fig. 4 is not good, indicating that the deformation conditions have a significant effect on the dynamic recrystallization activation energy. Therefore, the calculation results of the activation energy for dynamic recrystallization above are only the average value within the experimental range of this paper. Zener and Hollomon [11] studied the constitutive relationship of steel and found that rheological stress depends on temperature and strain rate. Therefore, they proposed the Zener-Hollomon (Z) parameter model to characterize the comprehensive influence of T and ε\_ on the rheological stress.

According to this method, the material constant C under other T and ε\_ can also be obtained.

caption: Fig. 7 e Dynamic recrystallization state diagram for the steel under hot deformation.
citation_sentences: According to the relationship between $\varepsilon _ { \mathrm { { C } } } , \varepsilon _ { \mathrm { { s } } }$ and the Z parameter above, the dynamic recrystallization state diagram of hot deformation of $\mathrm { F e { \mathrm { - } } M n { \mathrm { - } } A l { \mathrm { - } } C }$ steel can be drawn, as shown in Fig. 7, in which area A is the zone of work hardening, area B is the zone of partial DRX and area C is the zone of complete DRX. As it can be seen from Fig. 7, with the increase of the Z parameter, that is, with the increase of ε\_ or the decrease of T, the $\varepsilon _ { \mathrm { { C } } }$ and $\varepsilon _ { s }$ of dynamic recrystallization increases. It indicates that the behavior of starting DRX and completing DRX of the steel has become more difficult. The above experimental phenomena have reproducibility.

caption: Fig. 8 e Different deformation zones for the sample after compression: 1-difficult deformation zone; 2-small deformation zone; 3-large deformation zone.
citation_sentences: During hot deformation, the microstructure of DRX directly affects the grain size of the forgings and then affects the mechanical properties of the materials. Due to the friction between the contact surface and the pressure head during compression, the stress in the material is unevenly distributed, leading to different deformation zones in the section parallel to the loading direction of the compression sample, as shown in Fig. 8. Among them, 1 is a difficult deformation zone, 2 is a small deformation zone and 3 is a large deformation zone. To comprehensively analyze the evolution law of microstructure of FeeMneAleC steel during hot deformation, the microstructure of three deformation zones in Fig. 8 was observed. The results are shown in Fig. 9 (1, 2 and 3 in Fig. 9 represent the corresponding microstructure of the zone of difficult deformation, the zone of small deformation and the zone of large deformation, respectively).

Figure 10 shows the dynamic recrystallization average grain size of the steel under different T and ε\_ when the hot compression reaches 60%. The microstructure of the measured grain size is taken from the core of the compressed sample, which is the large deformation zone. Figure 10 shows that the DRX grain size of the steel after hot deformation increases with the decrease of ε\_ or the increase of T. Among them, when the T is $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the ε\_ is $0 . 0 1 { \mathsf { s } } ^ { - 1 }$ , the grain size exceeds $7 0 \ \mu \mathrm { m } ,$ and the structure is coarse grain, indicating mthat the recrystallized grains under this condition have grown rapidly. However, at low T (900e950 -C) and high ε\_ $_ { ( 1 - 1 0 \thinspace s ^ { - 1 } ) }$ , the dynamic recrystallization grains are small, but not sufficient and the original grains with large size remained (Fig. 9 (d)), and the microstructure homogeneity was poor.

caption: Fig. 10 e Dynamic recrystallization average grain size of the steel under different T and ε\_. LevenbergeMarquardt optimization algorithm was used to perform the mathematical regression analysis on the experimental data, and the prediction model for the average grain size of the dynamic recrystallization of the low-density highstrength steel was obtained: $$ d _ {\text { rex }} = 8 0 7 7 4 3. 6 3 \dot {\varepsilon} ^ {- 0. 1 9 0 3} \exp (- 1 2 0 6 4 8. 4 7 / R T) + 4. 3 3 8 \tag {12} $$

caption: Fig. 11 e The correlation and absolute error between predicted and experimental values of dynamic recrystallization grain size model.
citation_sentences: During the dynamic recrystallization of the material, the grain size constantly changes under the driving force. On the one hand, the grain growth is promoted under the action of temperature and plastic strain and the driving force has a positive effect on the grain size. On the other hand, dynamic recrystallization refines the microstructure inside the material and the driving force is a counter-driving force, which inhibits the grain growth [27e29]. This may cause errors in the dynamic recrystallization average grain size prediction model. In addition, there were some statistical errors. The results are shown in Fig. 11, R is 0.996 and MRE for all samples is only 4.53%, indicating a high degree of coincidence. Figure 11 (c) shows that the absolute error between the predicted value of the grain size and the experimental value is mostly within 3 m, which further shows that the model can accurately predict the dynamic recrystallization average grain size of low-density high-strength steel. It provides a reliable theoretical basis for the prediction of the dynamic recrystallized grain size of the steel after hot forging.
